body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

body.title: Design Team Report on DTLS for SCTP
Design Team Report on DTLS for SCTP

transcript.text: Okay, it's four o'clock. Let's start. Do we have uh can I get a show of hands of people who were not here for the session 30 minutes ago quickly? Uh a couple people. All right. If if for some reason you need to you're not feeling the note, well, go check it. I'm not going to run through that again. This is functionally the same session. We're going to jump right into it. Moit. >> Hello everyone. I'm Moit. Uh I'll be talking about and giving an update on the uh internet draft on flow Q pi. So uh the overview of the draft is that it combines flow queuing and the pi algorithm pretty much similar to how fqodel combines flow queuing with codel and the functioning of fq is pretty similar to rfc 8290 which is fqodel and the functioning of pi is similar to rfc 8033. However, there is a small modification that we suggest in our draft that is we recommend to use uh timestamps instead of using little slot to calculate the Q delay. And there are currently three implementations of FQI. U one of the implementations is in Linux. There's a separate implementation in FreeBSD and we also have an implementation of FQI in NS3. So if we look at the similar kind of AQM algorithms like codel and fqodel we notice that we have uh specifications for all of them codal fqc codel and pi u fqi specification has been missing and hence we actually wrote this draft. Uh there have been three versions of this draft. The first 00 draft described the algorithm. uh in the next version of it we updated the scope of experimentation uh by adding uh scenarios from RFC 7928. RFC 7928 actually tells us what are the different tests that should be performed uh when we try to you know propose a new algorithm and the latest draft that we updated before this meeting adds text about uh the freeBSD implementation of FQI which was missing in the previous two uh versions of the draft. Uh so far we have collaborated with uh uh two entities for testing the deployment of FQI in real world. Uh one is a company called quantum networks. Uh that's a website and they have an access point uh QQI235 wherein they have provided this access point and we have been testing FQI and we have shared the results of uh the functioning to the company and we are looking forward for more updates from their side. Uh we also reached out to uh LibraQS and uh they have uh shown willingness to add the support of FQI in the next releases. Uh we are also having a discussion about this in the uh Zulip chat. So please feel free to join there and discuss about it. And we are also looking forward for uh more collaborations if somebody would be uh interested to help us in testing uh real deployment. And with that uh I'll stop and just maybe request the TSVWG community to know whether this is the right time to probably go for the adoption of this draft. I'm thank you for preparing this draft and and presenting it. Um as you pointed out in the earlier slide, um this does fill a gap in the uh RFC series um with respect to some of the the available uh FQ AQM QIS. So I think it's a worthwhile thing for us to adopt it personally. Um actually had one technical comment as well. U go back to slide two. Um so um you made a a minor modification to AD33 um using timestamps. Um that actually um raises sort of an interesting um uh trade-off in the implementation of um of an AQM. Um uh so uh in the implementation you have generally a choice of uh doing your AQM action at NQ or at DQ. Um and in PI it's generally done at NQ. Um >> uh the downside to doing it at NQ is that your congestion signal goes into the end of the queue effectively, right? And so it has to so it's delayed by the Q depth before it you transit on to the receiver and eventually back to sender. Yeah. >> Uh but you also have a choice in how you um uh make your drop decisions and what information you use to do that. Um and the two main ways are either so time calculation using timestamps to effectively measure the Q delay after the fact. Yeah. >> So kind of delayed measurement or Q delay prediction which is you know at the NQ time how much delay do I think this packet will experience you know before it it it has forwarded on and back in the discussions in the AQM working group about codle and pi um both of them sort of balanced each other out in terms of how they manage that. So codle was um doing so and time calculation. is kind of a delayed measurement, but the congestion signal was uh provided right at your transmit time effectively, right? The drops were done at at DQ and PI did it the opposite. And in effect, they were kind of balanced, right? Um but if you if you change PI to instead use old information for the control law um and you put the congestion signal, you know, in the queue in effect, you're kind of, you know, delaying that uh that process. I wonder if that's been studied um what what impact that has on the performance. I mean obviously the control law is has got some um some smoothing of the information a bit right with the um the the I part of the the PI control um >> uh and the update only happens periodically but just um curious if that's something that's been studied. Uh yes there is a there is a study paper that I have also provided a link to in my draft where we actually reported an issue with the Linux kernel implementation of pi particularly when use when it was using the Q delay estimator instead of using the sojun time and um we actually fixed that bug in the kernel and its upstream already from last couple of years. So the Linux kernel today actually does the soen time calculation in pi and not the estimator. So there's a full article that we published in computer networks journal explaining about the study that we have performed. >> Okay. And in that paper it was studied that you know the the impact of delaying the uh the latency estimate. >> Um not from that perspective but we compared both the techniques that which one will be actually uh providing you better control in terms of latency whether you go with the sojunent time estimates or with the um estimator the little the little slaw estimator. Right. Right. >> Okay. All right. Thank you. Sure. >> So um I think last time in was it Bangkok we were kind of talking a little bit of TSV WG was the right home and after a extensive conversation with the CCWG chairs and our ad the answer is yes. So um if this was going to be adopted somewhere it would be here and we'll ask the questions at this time. >> All right. So I think uh we it has been there for like this is the third time you're presenting right so uh as we decided like TBWG the home so let's um and we got a good comments sometimes like so it it is something perhaps very good thing to do but first thing first we would like to know like how many of you have read the document if you can use the tool to vote >> it's a good opportunity it's a an opportunity for you to scan the QR code that's hanging on the mics if you haven't already. I think it's stabilized there. All right. Oh, yes. I want to say >> yeah. >> Is anyone still struggling with the tool? It needs more time. >> Okay. Should we kill it? >> Yeah, let's kill it. Okay. No one raising their hands here. So, I'm going to kill it. Let the record show that seven people claim to have read it, 23 no, and two people don't remember if they read it. Okay. Okay. Maybe we are having lack of comments today because people still want to read it. So I I think I think we should we should do I mean we can we can go for this other call. I mean >> yeah. So, so, so people people may not have read it, but I think our at least by now understand what this draft is about. So, maybe we ask the question as always we'll confirm on the list. >> Yeah. >> Okay, let's ask the question then. That was a very good presentation because many people who did not read it think it should be adopted. Does anyone need more time? Uh, no one was opposed and eight people had no excuse me, eight people had no opinion. As always, we'll take this a list to confirm, but that's a pretty positive strong signal. >> Yeah, thank you. >> Thank you. >> All right. Uh, our next presentation is the last one of the day, which may not take us to the next 48 minutes. It is the report. Just for those of you who might be a little bit uh rusty on what was going on here, we have a deliverable to 3GPP for DTLS over SCTP. The STP community was quite divided for quite some time about how to achieve this and we tked the design team to go work out the differences and find a common approach and uh this is the result and they've been working hard meaning a lot since Bangkok and uh the two of the proponents here are going to now present on the result of the design team for the working group's consideration. >> Let me give you slide control and go. >> Okay. I'm Magnus Westland and Michael here we're gonna talk about this. Um so um so um as Martin said the assign team was kind of locked in to find an uh some compromise by this meeting or Martin threatened with other procedures. Um um so but the design team has managed to come to a compromise and have a proposal for uh from this design team to present that's we're going to present and see that if we can adopt that proposal and going forward with it. Um so yeah so this is a picture showing the architecture we agreed on. It has a couple of colors which uh I will explain. So uh a DTLS implementation is an important part of this architecture and uh we have one functional unit in the DTLS in a DTLS implementation. We want to f uh I want to describe here and this is what we call uh DTLS record protector. So DTLS has the DTLS packets are called a record and they have a sequence number and they get on the sender side they get assigned a sequence number they get encrypted and sent. So that's the sender side of a DTLS record protector. On the receiver side of a record protector it um uh decrypts the packet and runs uh a replay protection. So it makes sure that a packet with a certain sequence number is only processed once. This is the receiver side of the DTLS record protector. And what you can see here is uh on the upper right um what we call a key management implementation. This consists of the yellow part which is a a DTLS implementation and let's say from a functional point of view unmodified DTLS implementation DTLS 1.3 and uh the only upper layer for that DTLS implementation is a small uh functional unit running the post handshake authentication which is already defined in an RFC and it runs messages over uh DTLS. Um the lower part, the lower blue part is an HTTP implementation and what you see here is that you have also a red box which is called DTLS chunk protector and this is functionally equivalent to the DTLS record protector but it has not user um it has different payload and it has its own set of parameters. So it has its own replay window. It has its own sequence number, its own epoch, its own variables. But if you have a userland SCTP stack, it allows you to use the same implementation of the record protector for both functions. If you have a kernel stack and a kernel SCTP stack and a userland DTLS stack, you have to replicate this function in the SCTP kernel. We have here green arrows. The green arrows are basically how key material is passed. So we have a key management um block in the DTLS implementation and it talks to the DTLS record layer to give the record protection uh record protector the encrypt uh the the secrets and and other cryptographic material to process and it provides the key exporter with information such that you can export uh also um the secrets And these exported secrets are then used for the DTLS chunk protector. So the record protect DTLS record protector and the DTLS chunk protector are using different key material. Um and this all is triggered by the upper layer protocol. Also the upper layer protocol can trigger um that a post handshake authentication has to run or a DTLS connection needs to be set up or a rekeying has to happen uh an extended key update pro uh thing. The key management implementation talks to the SCTP implementation basically giving uh all the parameters needed for the DTLS chunk protector. So these are the keys. Now start a new epoch. Remove the keys from an old epoch. This kind of stuff. And uh since you have data going from HTTP to the upper layer protocol or from HTTP to the uh key management implementation, this gets multiplexed over SCTP and demultiplex by the payload protocol identifier. So you can have both directions. >> All right. Two clarifying questions. Uh the post handshake authentication refers to which handshake is STP DTLS >> uh DTLS. >> Okay, thanks. And did I hear you correctly that really the only non like offtheshelf code here is the DTLS chunk protector assuming you can't just >> Yes, that's what I said. Well, I I was formulating a functional unmodified um uh implementation. So the point is that um the DTLS implementation needs to trigger the key exporter and have the interaction with the SCTP level. This might not be in an offthe-shelf DTLS implementation. So you need to to have the hooks for the API, but it's functionally unmodified assuming that you have the uh the extended key update implemented which is currently under specification in the TLS working group. John >> uh regarding the post handshake authentication, I see that TU has a new draft 00 draft in TLS saying that this is a new draft needed to update the certificate using this uh exporter. This seems to indicate that tu thinks that the current solution is not able to update the certificate and that are we now like depending on some new 00 graft. >> No, as far as my understand is ti here can he? So so my so my understanding is that uh the post handshake authentication runs on top of DTLS. That's an RFC. That's the solution we have right now. That's the solution which goes into the key management document. Um there is work what you're referring to to do the post handshake authentication in the DTLS layer. >> Okay. >> And that might be standardized in the future. And if that is the case, you can modify this key management implement. You can modify this key management solution and we have already in the in the SCTP handshake the facility to negotiate multiple key management solutions. So we can update the document in the future using that internal staff and we are we have the the the facilities to negotiate that. In the case the key management uh document is delayed for whatever reasons and the other document is very fast it could be integrated but it's not that we are waiting for that. Okay, let's continue into some other details now that we have gone through the high level kind of picture how things fits together. Um so uh on the uh on the detailer chunk um so kind of design principles that we try to there here on these two things is the detail chunk we want to be able to use reuse the as we said existing record processing in in detail as implementations. uh when you do an userland STP stack um we want to have very clear and simple interaction models with key management and with STP stack and we want to support alternative key management uh because in some sense we already have it uh requirements on that we have discussed the design team um and we try to keep the impact of the STP stack low uh so uh and we try to use the facilities exist in STP like parameters for the unit act to uh signal key management solutions which we're going to basically call it called protection solution indicators in this slide set. So uh on the key management as before we said we're going to use detailers 1.3 connections as the basis. This post hunt uh handshake reauthentication is based on RC 9261. It uses uh TLS extended key update to generate new master secrets to derive keys from keying. Um you using PP IDs to separate the key management traffic. uh and we also have a new way of trying to address downgrade protection based on the protection solution and the fairs in key revision. We'll go into more detail about that in coming slides. Uh and the message exchange for the key management is solely on top of STP using in order reliable uh messages. So >> just wanted to say that you're on p give based on slide count you were on pace to use the entire time for the presentation itself. So >> I just hopefully it goes a little faster. >> Yeah. Yeah it will go faster but uh we go more so but um around these hooks etc. I just want to show how you actually arrive at keys etc. So we start there on the top with the SCTP normal for handshake and in this initial you do send the parameter says I want I support the following uh protection solutions and you have an preference and that's in a preference ordered list the pair selects which of them and return that parameter back um and then you can conclude on the STP establishment. So both sides then get saying okay what was the protection solution indicator vector so saying the list that was in the init uh needs to be learned by the I layer on both sides I mean you first know what is put in and the second need to get it out and then it's uh in this example we triggering the hand crypto handshakes the key management handshakes by the STP established completion and then you send the DTLS handshakes. So when you gotten to a point where you have keys, you start driving them. But here you do have some uh you see this purple text which is start setting the keys into the detail chunk. And this needs to be done in a certain order so you don't deadlock yourself if because you can have need for retransmitting uh these STP message before you're starting to actually so you're sending uh you have the send and receive keys on the client side. Uh but then you send a search response and the detail is finished to the to the peer. At that point you only set the receive keys and when the other side then has received and you know he will have received keys installed I can send encrypted details shank now so that's why the details act that goes back there is actually protected by details chunk that's what's the brackets surrounded this um and to do that it just sets the send and receive keys for this session hello association uh and when you come back to that side he's received secured message knows that oh now I can actually set uh protection and also sets the send key so any future now will be protected from a side and he will require the other side to send also encrypted uh at this point the uh any future data exchange or based on sender dry the other pair can tell it's lower detail chunk saying from this point only accept protected the details uh protected STP packets. So that is how we establish the sessions. Um so um parameter protection solutions indicating what's what's uh which solutions which key management solutions you support also if you would have another secured solution that you would like to offer and and have choice between you could still use this parameter to indicate that I support them etc. Um um and as I said you responder picks a single solution. So the downgrade um what we've done here is try to prevent an attacker from modifying the unencrypted unprotected initial handshake. uh and to ensure that you don't get downgrade in this phase we're actually going to do so that um the when we derive the keys we're actually going to take this PSI vectors and input into the key derivation. So when you run the TLS exporter you input this data in com combined with how your particular key management and if you do that uh if not both side has the same PSI vector you will fail. So that way you can protect uh from the no extra SS changes, nothing of that. Uh you're not vulnerable to the weakest solution being possible to compromise. However, you will not get the clear signal that there was a downgrade, but you get this benefit that it's harder to attack. So the details chunk is an STP chunk with uh and details record inside. using as much of the detail as record for identifying uh these uh so you have the STP association you know that you have a it's a details chunk itself identifying and then you will have a details record inside which will start with this header depicted on the lower left side. So um so that way we can see we have the association and then you try to identify the key context from association the connection ID if it's included the epoch which is the lower two bits is e on the first bite and then sequence number is what you then determine okay this particular full context Next special information is what's being decrypted. So you get the right and packets. Yeah. Yeah. So this is the uh an overview about the key management and you see on the right side um well the ASI figure from the key management document which is uh very similar to to the architectural one. Um so you can see you have the handshake um protocol running inside the DTLS and the application protocol. Um we actually for the initial key establishment we run a normal DTLS handshake. Um the DTLS handshake will also derive which cryptographic algorithms will be used for DT for the DTLS communication but it's the same which is then used for the DTLS chunk. So it's uh they are not using different cryptographic algorithms and there is no SCTP specific negotiation of that. The re keying is done with a extended key update. We need an extended key update because we derive the keys for the DTLS chunk from the key exporter and the current key exchange in DTLS does not update uh what is used for the key exporter. So that's why we need um the extended uh key update for the reauthentication. we use something which is running on top of DTLS uh which is a post handshake authentication um uh we need to define there the precise messages to be put in there but the the procedure is uh defined we don't need any specification for that yes so we have um um defined for the uh for the HTTP chunk um for for the DTLS chunk uh an API. So we have focused on uh what you what you need to provide. Uh this means you must be able to identify um what key material you want to use in which thing. So for example for a particular association for a particular epoch and so on. And um this then needs to be updated when we run uh the key uh extended key update. SAP restart is an issue. Um we uh this is happening. So so this is basically uh two sides communicate, one site dies um comes up again and wants to reestablish the communication while the peer has not even detected that uh the site went down. um this feature is needed in this um uh in this uh application area uh and we want to make sure that this can't be used by an attacker to uh to take over an association. The figure shown here indicates that uh this new handshake is uh also protected. Um I think this will change in the next uh revision because um this the SCP specification uh requires you to use zero as the verification tag often packet containing an init chunk and uh middle boxes and firewalls are required to enforce this by dropping any packet with a with a verification tag of zero and not having an init chunk. And this would look like on the wire you see an HTTP common header and a DTLS chunk and this combination wouldn't work. So the first two messages would be unprotected. Um but that's not not a security issue. Uh yeah. Um there is also um the um um the uh the wish to perform this restart um pretty fast. So you have to go through the HTTP handshake. The the idea is not to go through the DTLS handshake. So when a DTLS connection is set up set up, a specific restart key material is um uh generated and can be used when restarting. On the server side, this means you need to store another key as you already do. That's nothing special.
